source_paper_title: Fair-Share Allocations for Agents with Arbitrary Entitlements
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Babaioff_2024, title={Fair-Share Allocations for Agents with Arbitrary Entitlements}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2021.0199}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2021.0199}, number={4}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Babaioff, Moshe and Ezra, Tomer and Feige, Uriel}, year={2024}, month=nov, pages={2180–2211} }
context:
Page 5, Introduction (paragraph after Theorem 1.3); reiterated page 5 (after Theorem 1.4) and page 23, Section 6.3 (Open Questions, items 2–3).

As the APS is at least as large as the MMS, we thus conclude that when there are more than two agents one cannot hope to give all agents their APS share exactly, even in the equal entitlements case, and constant approximation is the best we can hope for (we leave open the problem of finding the exact constant).

Finding the best approximation is open even for the case of equal entitlements.
title: Determine optimal approximation constant for AnyPrice share allocations with additive valuations
background_latex:
There are \(n\) agents \(N=\{1,\dots,n\}\) and a set \(M\) of \(m\) indivisible goods. Each agent \(i\) has an additive valuation \(v_i:2^M\to\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}\) of the form \(v_i(S)=\sum_{j\in S} v_i(j)\). Agents have entitlements (budgets) \(b_i>0\) with \(\sum_{i\in N} b_i = 1\).

For a fixed agent \(i\), define the set of nonnegative price vectors with total price 1 as
\[P=\{(p_1,\dots,p_m)\in\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}^m: \sum_{j\in M} p_j = 1\}.\]
The AnyPrice share (APS) of agent \(i\) is
\[\mathrm{APS}_i \;=\; \min_{p\in P}\; \max_{S\subseteq M\, :\, \sum_{j\in S} p_j\le b_i} v_i(S).\]
(Equivalently, \(\mathrm{APS}_i\) is the largest \(z\) for which there exists a distribution over bundles \(T\subseteq M\) each of value at least \(z\), such that each item \(j\in M\) appears with total probability at most \(b_i\).)

An allocation is a partition \(A=(A_1,\dots,A_n)\) of \(M\) into disjoint bundles. For \(\alpha\in(0,1]\), an \(\alpha\)-APS allocation is an allocation \(A\) such that \(v_i(A_i)\ge \alpha\,\mathrm{APS}_i\) for every agent \(i\in N\).

For additive valuations and arbitrary entitlements, the paper proves existence and polynomial-time computability of \(\alpha\)-APS allocations for \(\alpha=3/5\) (and stronger entitlement-dependent bounds), and notes that exact \(\alpha=1\) is impossible in general when \(n\ge 3\), even under equal entitlements.
problem_definition_latex:
Determine the largest constant \(\alpha^\star\in(0,1)\) such that, for every instance with additive valuations \((v_1,\dots,v_n)\) and entitlements \((b_1,\dots,b_n)\) with \(\sum_i b_i=1\), there exists an allocation \(A=(A_1,\dots,A_n)\) satisfying
\[\forall i\in N:\quad v_i(A_i)\ge \alpha^\star\,\mathrm{APS}_i,\]
where \(\mathrm{APS}_i\) is defined by
\[\mathrm{APS}_i \;=\; \min_{p\in P}\; \max_{S\subseteq M\, :\, \sum_{j\in S} p_j\le b_i} v_i(S).\]
Equivalently, determine the optimal worst-case approximation factor for simultaneously guaranteeing each agent a fixed constant fraction of her AnyPrice share (for additive valuations), and provide matching upper and lower bounds (and, if possible, a polynomial-time algorithm achieving \(\alpha^\star\)).
latex:

location_in_paper: Page 5, Introduction (paragraph after Theorem 1.3); reiterated page 5 (after Theorem 1.4) and page 23, Section 6.3 (Open Questions, items 2–3).
open_problem_quote:
As the APS is at least as large as the MMS, we thus conclude that when there are more than two agents one cannot hope to give all agents their APS share exactly, even in the equal entitlements case, and constant approximation is the best we can hope for (we leave open the problem of finding the exact constant).

Finding the best approximation is open even for the case of equal entitlements.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: Upper bounds can be sought by searching for small instances (few agents/items) where no allocation achieves a target \(\alpha\), but certifying the universal impossibility of \(\alpha\) typically requires nontrivial reasoning beyond brute-force search. Still, automated enumeration with exact APS computation (pseudo-polynomial/ILP) can generate candidate hard instances and empirical gaps.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate objects include both (i) algorithm families/strategies parameterized by thresholds and (ii) adversarial instances parameterized by item values and entitlements. A fitness signal can be computed by solving, for each generated instance, the best achievable minimum ratio \(\min_i v_i(A_i)/\mathrm{APS}_i\) over allocations (via MILP/branch-and-bound for small sizes) and, for algorithms, the achieved ratio on sampled/adversarially optimized instances, enabling iterative improvement and counterexample discovery.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The AnyPrice Share (APS) benchmark of Babaioff–Ezra–Feige is defined for each agent \(i\) with entitlement \(b_i\) as \(\mathrm{APS}_i=\min_{p\in P}\max\{v_i(S):\sum_{j\in S}p_j\le b_i\}\), equivalently as the largest \(z\) for which there exists a distribution over bundles of value at least \(z\) where each item appears with total probability at most \(b_i\). For additive valuations, the source paper proves a universal constant guarantee: there is always a polynomial-time computable allocation giving every agent at least \(3/5\) of her APS (and in fact \(\max\{3/5,1/(2-b_i)\}\) depending on entitlement). It also shows exact \(\alpha=1\) is impossible for \(n\ge 3\) even under equal entitlements, so the central question is to pin down the optimal universal constant \(\alpha^\star\in(0,1)\) for additive valuations and arbitrary entitlements.

Subsequent work in the provided forward citations does not close this gap. "Weighted fairness notions for indivisible items revisited" consolidates the state of the art: it reiterates the \(3/5\) lower bound and points to an impossibility barrier inherited from maximin-share (MMS) lower bounds, namely that no \(\alpha\)-APS allocation exists for any \(\alpha>39/40\) (via instances where no allocation gives everyone more than \(39/40\) of MMS, and \(\mathrm{APS}\ge \mathrm{MMS}\)). Its main new APS-related contribution is restricted-domain: for binary additive valuations, the weighted egalitarian (WEG) rule is APS-fair (achieves \(\alpha=1\) for APS). A separate line, "Almost (Weighted) Proportional Allocations for Indivisible Chores**", studies chores and shows WPROPX implies a factor-2 approximation to a chore-analog of APS; while not directly improving goods-APS constants, it suggests that price-based and proportionality-up-to-one-item techniques can yield APS-type guarantees in other manna models.

Overall, based on the cited literature here, the optimal constant \(\alpha^\star\) for additive valuations and arbitrary entitlements remains open: the best universal lower bound is \(3/5\) (with better entitlement-dependent guarantees), and the best stated universal upper bound is \(39/40\) (inherited from MMS impossibility for equal entitlements, hence applying to APS as well). Narrowing the gap likely requires either (i) new impossibility constructions tailored to APS (not just MMS), or (ii) improved algorithms/rounding arguments that exploit the APS dual (distributional) characterization beyond the current bidding-game analysis.

sources.title: Weighted fairness notions for indivisible items revisited
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper does not improve the known universal constant for APS with additive valuations. It reiterates the existence of a \(3/5\)-APS allocation (from Babaioff et al. 2021b) and cites the upper bound that no \(\alpha\)-APS allocation exists for any \(\alpha>39/40\) (from Feige et al. 2021). It also proves that the weighted egalitarian (WEG) rule yields APS-fairness in the restricted domain of binary additive valuations (Corollary B.5): with binary valuations, every WEG allocation gives each agent value at least \(\lfloor w_i\,|Z_i|\rfloor\), hence is APS-fair and OMMS-fair.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for the optimal worst-case approximation factor \(\alpha^\star\) for guaranteeing each agent a constant fraction of her AnyPrice share (APS) under additive valuations and arbitrary entitlements. This paper is about weighted fairness notions and includes APS as one benchmark, but it does not derive new lower bounds (algorithms) or new impossibility bounds for \(\alpha\)-APS in the general additive setting. Its only direct APS-related new contribution is for a special case (binary additive valuations), where it shows WEG allocations are APS-fair (i.e., achieve \(\alpha=1\) for APS) in that restricted domain. For general additive valuations, it merely summarizes the state of the art: \(3/5\) achievable and \(>39/40\) impossible.

sources.title: Almost (Weighted) Proportional Allocations for Indivisible Chores✱✱
sources.theorem_or_result: For additive chore costs with arbitrary obligation shares \((s_i)\), the paper proves existence and polynomial-time computability of weighted proportionality up to any item (WPROPX) allocations, and shows that any WPROPX allocation is a 2-approximation to the chore-version of AnyPrice Share (APS): for every agent \(i\), \(c_i(X_i)\le 2\,\mathrm{APS}_i\) (Theorem 4.2 via Lemma 4.3).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for the optimal constant \(\alpha^\star\) guaranteeing \(v_i(A_i)\ge \alpha^\star\,\mathrm{APS}_i\) for additive valuations over indivisible goods with arbitrary entitlements. This paper studies indivisible chores (cost minimization) and a different fairness notion (PROPX/WPROPX), then derives a 2-approximation to a chore-adapted APS benchmark. It does not improve the best-known constant for APS allocations for goods (e.g., \(3/5\) in Babaioff–Ezra–Feige) nor provide upper bounds for \(\alpha^\star\) in the goods setting. Its main relevance is as a related technique/implication: WPROPX \(\Rightarrow\) 2-APS for chores, giving a constant-factor APS guarantee (factor 2) but in the opposite-sign utility model and with a different APS definition (max–min form for chores).

sources.title: Fair division of indivisible goods: Recent progress and open questions
sources.theorem_or_result:
This paper is a survey and does not prove new approximation guarantees for AnyPrice share (APS). It summarizes known results, including that for additive valuations with arbitrary entitlements there is a polynomial-time algorithm guaranteeing a 
3/5
-fraction of each agent's APS (citing Babaioff et al. 2021b).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for the optimal worst-case constant \(\alpha^\star\) such that an \(\alpha^\star\)-APS allocation always exists for additive valuations and arbitrary entitlements, ideally with matching upper/lower bounds and an algorithm. The citing paper does not address this optimization question; it only provides context and a literature overview, restating the known \(3/5\) guarantee and related weighted-fairness notions (WEF1, WMMS, etc.). No improved lower bound, upper bound, or impossibility for APS is established here.

sources.title: Fair division of indivisible goods: A survey
sources.theorem_or_result: This paper is a survey and does not prove new approximation guarantees for AnyPrice share (APS). It summarizes known results in discrete fair division with additive valuations, including the result of Babaioff et al. [2021b] that for arbitrary entitlements there is a polynomial-time computable allocation giving each agent at least \(3/5\) of her APS.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for the optimal constant \(\alpha^\star\) guaranteeing an \(\alpha\)-APS allocation for all additive instances with arbitrary entitlements, and ideally matching upper/lower bounds and algorithms. The citing paper does not improve the \(3/5\) guarantee, provide impossibility examples tightening the upper bound, or otherwise analyze \(\alpha^\star\). It only provides contextual background by describing APS and citing the \(3/5\)-APS existence/algorithm result from the original APS paper.
